attributes.stem: A previously well 54-year-old man presents with a three day history of intermittent right loin pain which has become constant over the last 24 hours. He has vomited once and has never had this sort of pain before. On examination he appears uncomfortable, with a blood pressure of 115/75 mmHg, pulse 110/min and temperature 38.6°C. His abdomen is soft, apart from tenderness in the right flank. A CT scan shows a 7mm stone at the right pelvic brim and a moderate degree of dilatation of the right pelvicalyceal system. He is admitted for analgesia and intravenous antibiotics.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's clinical picture of severe unilateral and colicky flank pain is characteristic of renal calculus disease. This is confirmed with the CT scan which showed evidence of a stone in the ureter and from its site, resulting partial renal tract obstruction.

The treatment choice of nephrolithiasis depends on stone size, location, patient comorbidities and renal anatomy. In this clinical scenario, there is an obstructing stone and the patient has a fever. This is indicative of impending sepsis. The patient needs an immediate urological opinion and appropriate intervention to overcome the obstruction. Urine flow must be re-established promptly to minimise the risks of urosepsis and possible progression to multi-organ failure. The most effective way of overcoming the obstruction will be insertion of a ureteric stent to re-establish urine flow.

In other circumstances, ureteric stones 4 mm or less, will usually pass spontaneously and those 5-7 mm may still pass of their own accord, but the success rate falls to less than 60%. Larger stones will usually require some form of intervention.

If the patient did not have any suggestion of urinary obstruction or potential sepsis and whose symptoms could be well managed with analgesics, then continued conservative management would be appropriate. If the patient was symptom-free and did not have any evidence of obstruction with superimposed infection and the stone appeared to be placed in within the renal pelvis or a calyx, endoscopic shockwave lithotripsy might be appropriate if:

  • unexpected passage of the stone put the patient's life - or those of others - in jeopardy (eg airline pilot)

  • the kidney was a solitary one

  • the stone <2cm

  • comorbidities existed that might predispose to renal insufficiency (eg hypertension)

Laser lithotripsy might be considered in the patient with a stone at any site in the urinary tract, but not causing obstruction or sepsis. Percutaneous nephrostomy could be considered in the situation of an obstructed renal system where a stent could not be placed.
learningPoints: Sepsis in the presence of known renal stone disease raises the possibility of obstruction and some form of urinary tract decompression will be required.
references: Assimos D, Krambeck A, Miller NL, et al. Surgical Management of Stones: American Urological Association/Endourological Society Guideline, PART II. J Urol. 2016; 196:1161–69.
Corbo J, Wang J. Kidney and Ureteral Stones. Emerg Med Clin N Am 2019, 37:637–48
